result:
I’ve been thinking about how every object in my apartment is quietly aging.  
The cotton dish-towels are growing thinner in exactly the spots where my hands always grab them; the enamel kettle is yellowing the way old love letters do, from the burner’s rim upward like a slow sunrise in reverse. Even the silicone spatula is acquiring a ghost-scar, a pale outline of scrambled eggs that no dishwasher cycle will pardon. Nothing dramatic—no cracks, no clangorous breakdowns—just a soft, cellular surrender that you notice only when you return from a week away and find the place has kept breathing without you, exhaling microscopic flecks of itself into the dust motes.

I like to imagine that when I’m not looking, the furniture practices small, unauthorized migrations. The reading chair edges three millimeters toward the window each afternoon, chasing the declining sun; the left sandal crawls under the couch to gossip with the dust bunnies about where I’ve walked all day. The apartment is compiling a secret map of my restlessness in nicks, fades, and fraying. It knows I don’t sit still; it records the evidence in upholstery.

Sometimes I feel I’m the unpaid intern of my own life—fetching the glass of water I meant to drink two hours ago, re-running the script of tomorrow’s email while stirring tonight’s onions, apologizing to the plant I forgot to rotate and now leans like a teenager refusing eye contact. The onions don’t care if the slices are symmetrical; the plant doesn’t accept apologies. Still, I offer both, because ritual is the cheapest form of control available to mammals who can’t hibernate.

Outside, the city performs its nightly magic trick: turning rain into neon. The gutters become fluid mirrors that double the traffic lights—green, yellow, red—so that every car appears to be driving twice, once on asphalt and once in liquid replica. I stand on the balcony and watch strangers scurry beneath this duplicated cosmos, each gripping an umbrella the way a knight once held a shield, and I wonder if they, too, feel like blurred copies of themselves, half here, half somewhere else they’d rather be.

I read once that the human heart tires in waves, like a lighthouse beam that turns and turns but sometimes wants to smash its own bulb against the rocks. Yet it doesn’t; it just keeps sweeping the shoreline, warning boats that stubbornly keep coming. My cardiologist says the irregular thump I feel is “benign.” A beautiful word—meaning harmless, but also: well-meaning, like a lie told by a friend who wants you to survive the night.

Survival, though, is a low bar. I want the highwire version: to walk across the canyon of Tuesday afternoon on a cable made of violin strings, humming something reckless and major-key while every below-average thought I’ve ever had waits like crocodiles in the ravine. I want to finish the novel I started in 2016, learn to fold dumplings so they don’t explode in the pot, and text my mother back before she resorts to sending weather screenshots as conversation. I want to be the person who drinks the glass of water promptly, who remembers to rotate the plant, who is not afraid of finishing things because finishing means choosing one life over all the others.

But tonight I will settle for smaller mercies: the sound of the kettle’s click, the way the dish-towel still absorbs even though it’s threadbare, the fact that the rain chooses to fall without asking permission. I will switch off the lights and let the apartment continue its slow, invisible craftsmanship: sculpting me into the shape of someone who lives here, one fray, one stain, one breath at a time.
